feedback.text: First of all, I would like to thank you for the opportunity of giving feedback to this act. I think the EU is doing a good work in keeping such processes democratic and that they listen actively to their citizens. I would like to say that I like this act and the idea behind it. I think that, if it passes, it can be a great protection for the people in Europe, and the 5 points are all very important. I would like to add one or two points tho, maybe they do not need to be separated and maybe they could also be put in a generalization of some others, as you prefer. I would like to focus more on consumer protection. The idea starts with the initiative "Stop killing games", but I think we can go a bit further. Many people are already asking to do something about the end-of-life plans of videogames, and I would like to be added to those people: we've seen games developed with a lot of care, such as "The Crew" from the company Ubisoft, disappearing. More insultingly, they also changed their user agreement to force people to destroy their physical copies of their games in case such games reach the end-of-life, and the company does not want them to be played anymore. I put the videogame "The Crew" as an example, but many more are in the same fate potentially. I think this is a plain insult to many people: first of all, the buyer of such videogames: after spending money for being able to play them, they are not able to play them anymore, just because they relied on the company infrastructure; however, this would be avoidable with an end-of-life plan: for instance, it could be possible to release part of the infrastructure to the public, without the needing to maintain it. Many companies, for instance, release the executables of their servers for multiplayer games. An example is Valve, with the "Counter-Strike" series, which lets anyone host their private servers. Then, I think it is an insult to the developers, who put care into a product that gets artificially destroyed, effectively rendering meaningless all their efforts to build a solid and enjoyable product. I chose the word "product" because it connects well with the next point: I would like all software needing a company infrastructure to work to have an end-of-life plan. For example, let us say that I purchased a version of a software that gets discontinued, and that is not able to communicate with the company servers anymore; however, such software could need a registration of sort, which is impossible to perform due to the server not being able to communicate anymore. In this case, an end-of-life plan could be distribuding a patch for such software to remove the registration step and enabling the usage for those who bought it; after all, if the software is at the end of its life, the company will not sell it anyways. This is an example, but it shows how digital products and consumer rights are at stake here. As a recap: I would like to add 2 points to the initiative: - First, some comsumer protection for videogames buyers and users, providing an end-of-life plan. - Second, some consumer protection for all software that can be bought, meaning that even after the end of support I should be able to use it. I would like to thank everyone for reading this and following to the end, and I hope those points are taken into consideration.
